body: A recurring theme in our speculative decoding work is that surprisingly simple interventions — vocabulary pruning, embedding-space probing, future contemplation — can yield significant inference speedups without complex training or architectural changes. I believe there is still a lot of low-hanging fruit in this space, and that principled analysis of why these simple methods work is as valuable as the speedups themselves.

topic: Control theory and RL as lenses for understanding LLMs
body: I am keen on exploring the application of control theory (focusing on stability and optimality guarantees) and reinforcement learning (data-driven methods) to enhance our understanding of large language models. While my current research is dedicated to LLMs, I maintain a strong interest in robotics and stay updated on the latest advancements in the field. Please feel free to reach out if you are interested in collaborating.

Diffusion-based language models are a genuinely interesting alternative to autoregressive generation, but inference efficiency is still an open problem. I've been thinking about how speculative decoding principles (draft-verify) can be adapted to continuous or masked diffusion, where the notion of a 'draft' is less obvious. The Spiffy and ConFu work are early steps here, but there's much more to explore at the intersection of the two paradigms.

topic: Combining VLAs with trajectory optimization and adaptive control
body: Vision-Language-Action models are increasingly capable at high-level task planning, but they largely sidestep the guarantees that classical trajectory optimization and adaptive control provide — stability, constraint satisfaction, robustness to parametric uncertainty. I'm interested in how these two worlds can be combined: using VLAs to specify goals and priors while letting principled control methods handle execution, especially in settings with dynamic or partially unknown environments.
